threats.title: Registry onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier Uses Wrong Boolean Logic, Bricking Vault Administration
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyVaultMaintainer modifier uses OR (||) in the revert condition: `if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false)`. This means it only passes when the caller is BOTH the vault maintainer AND holds the EMERGENCY_ROLE. In contrast, the onlyVaultGoverner modifier correctly uses AND (&&). The same bug exists in onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock. Functions gated by these modifiers (addConnector, removeTrustedPosition, updateConnectorTrustedTokens, addTrustedPosition) become inaccessible to their intended callers unless they hold both roles simultaneously.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable logic error that makes critical admin functions unreachable in normal operation, effectively bricking vault configuration. The maintainer address (a timelock contract) cannot add/remove connectors or update trusted tokens. Comparing to onlyVaultGoverner which uses && confirms the bug pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) - uses || instead of &&; compare to onlyVaultGoverner at lines 47-50 which correctly uses &&

preconditions: Vault must be created with different maintainer and emergency addresses (normal operation)
Caller must be the vault maintainer but NOT also hold EMERGENCY_ROLE

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: OR logic in revert condition requires BOTH conditions to pass instead of EITHER
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: onlyVaultMaintainer: `msg.sender != maintainer || !hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE)` reverts unless both are true; should be `msg.sender != maintainer && !hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE)`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No tests appear to verify that maintainer alone (without EMERGENCY_ROLE) can call gated functions

analysis_questions: Are there any deployment scripts that grant EMERGENCY_ROLE to the maintainer address?
Can the protocol function at all with this bug given the deployment configuration?
Does the onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock have the identical bug?
validation_requirements: Call addConnector as vault maintainer without EMERGENCY_ROLE - should revert
Call addConnector as EMERGENCY_ROLE holder without being maintainer - should also revert
Compare behavior with onlyVaultGoverner which uses correct logic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No attacker needed - protocol is self-bricked. The vault maintainer (intended to be a timelock) cannot manage connectors, trusted tokens, or trusted positions unless it also holds EMERGENCY_ROLE. This prevents normal vault administration.
prerequisites: Vault deployed with separate maintainer and emergency addresses (standard deployment)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete inability to manage vault connectors and positions; new integrations impossible, compromised connectors cannot be removed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors - vault becomes unmanageable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires redeployment of Registry contract or granting EMERGENCY_ROLE to maintainer (workaround)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for maintainer-only access (without emergency role) to gated functions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily verifiable by calling any gated function as maintainer

threats.title: Holding Position Index Corruption via Swap-and-Pop Key Mismatch Between msg.sender and calculatorConnector
threats.hypothesis_description: When a holding position is removed via swap-and-pop in updateHoldingPosition, the moved element's isPositionUsed key is recalculated using `calculatorConnector` (field from the struct). However, when positions are created, the key uses `msg.sender` (the calling connector). For token positions (type 0), calculatorConnector is the accountingManager while msg.sender is the connector—these differ. This creates orphaned entries in the isPositionUsed mapping, corrupting the position tracking system.
threats.investigation_rationale: The key mismatch causes the position index lookup to return incorrect values after any swap-and-pop involving token positions. This corrupts the entire position tracking system, potentially creating duplicate positions, making positions unremovable, and eventually exhausting maxNumHoldingPositions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Creation uses keccak256(abi.encode(msg.sender, _positionId, _data)) but swap-and-pop at lines 221-228 recalculates key using keccak256(abi.encode(holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, positionId, data))

preconditions: At least two holding positions exist in a vault
One position is removed triggering swap-and-pop (not the last element)
The moved position has calculatorConnector != the connector that created it (always true for token positions type 0)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Key derivation inconsistency: creation at line 213 uses msg.sender, swap-and-pop at line 222-226 uses calculatorConnector
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For _updateTokenInRegistry, calculatorConnector is accountingManager (from calculatePositionId) but msg.sender creating the position is the connector itself
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that the key reconstruction matches the original key

analysis_questions: How frequently does swap-and-pop actually occur in practice?
Which position types have calculatorConnector != creating connector?
Can the corruption cascade to block all position operations?
validation_requirements: Create multiple token positions from different connectors
Remove a position that is not the last element
Verify isPositionUsed mapping consistency after removal
Attempt to find/remove the moved position - should fail due to wrong key

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During normal vault operations, when connectors add/remove token positions, the swap-and-pop corrupts the index mapping. Over time, phantom entries accumulate, the maxNumHoldingPositions limit is hit with fewer real positions than expected, and some positions become permanently stuck.
prerequisites: Vault with multiple active token holding positions
Normal removal of any non-last position

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Position tracking corruption leads to inability to remove positions, potential duplicate entries, and eventual vault freeze when maxNumHoldingPositions is exhausted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Extremely difficult - requires manual reconstruction of position mappings or contract upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for position index consistency after swap-and-pop with heterogeneous position types

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Occurs during normal operations whenever positions are removed
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be detected by comparing isPositionUsed mapping with actual array contents

threats.title: TVL Calculation Underflow DoS in Multiple Connectors Freezes Entire Vault
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple connector _getPositionTVL functions perform unchecked subtraction of debt from collateral (e.g., `totalCollateral - totalDebt`). In Solidity 0.8.x, this reverts on underflow if debt exceeds collateral. Since TVLHelper.getTVL iterates ALL positions and calls each connector's getPositionTVL, a single underwater position reverts the entire TVL calculation, blocking all vault deposits, withdrawals, and fee calculations that depend on TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: Market conditions can easily push leveraged positions underwater (interest accrual, price drops, liquidation). The TVLHelper itself handles negative total TVL gracefully (returns 0), but individual connector TVL functions do not. This is a systemic DoS vector affecting any vault with lending positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tvl = totalDepositAmount - totalBAmount; unchecked subtraction reverts if totalBAmount > totalDepositAmount. Same pattern in PrismaConnector:160, CompoundConnector:108, Dolomite:91, AaveConnector:103

preconditions: Vault has at least one lending position (Silo, Prisma, Compound, Dolomite, or Aave)
The position becomes underwater (debt > collateral) due to market conditions, interest accrual, or partial liquidation

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: SiloConnector.sol line 103, PrismaConnector.sol line ~160, CompoundConnector.sol line ~108, Dolomite.sol line ~91, AaveConnector.sol line ~103
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked subtraction: collateral - debt without underflow protection
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Solidity 0.8.x reverts on underflow, propagating revert through TVLHelper.getTVL to all vault operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check for debt > collateral before subtraction; should return 0 in that case

analysis_questions: Which vault operations depend on TVLHelper.getTVL?
Can the underwater position be removed while TVL calculation is broken?
Is there an emergency bypass for TVL calculation?
validation_requirements: Create a lending position, then simulate market conditions where debt > collateral
Call TVLHelper.getTVL and confirm it reverts
Confirm that deposits and withdrawals are blocked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During market volatility, a vault's Aave/Compound/Silo position goes underwater. TVLHelper.getTVL reverts, freezing all deposits, withdrawals, and share price calculations. The vault is permanently locked until the position is manually brought above water, which may be impossible if the keeper cannot execute transactions due to the TVL calculation failure.
prerequisites: Active lending position in any of the affected connectors
Market conditions causing debt > collateral

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete vault freeze - no deposits or withdrawals possible, potentially trapping all user funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires external capital injection to bring position above water, or emergency action to remove the underwater position

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for underwater position TVL calculation behavior

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None for attack; recovery requires capital
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered by market conditions, not attacker action
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring health factors of lending positions

threats.title: UNIv3Connector TVL Overcounting via Aggregated Pool Position Instead of Per-NFT Position
threats.hypothesis_description: The _getPositionTVL function queries Uniswap V3 pool.positions() using `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU))` which returns the TOTAL aggregated liquidity from ALL NonfungiblePositionManager NFTs at that tick range, not just the vault's specific tokenId. Any other user with a position at the same tick range through the same positionManager inflates the vault's reported TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: The NonfungiblePositionManager consolidates all positions at the same tick range into a single pool-level position. The vault's TVL includes liquidity from all other users at that tick range. This directly inflates share prices, enabling value extraction by existing depositors at the expense of new depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 key = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU)); (uint128 liquidity,,, ...) = pool.positions(key); - queries aggregated position, not per-NFT liquidity

preconditions: Vault holds a Uniswap V3 position
Other users have positions through the same NonfungiblePositionManager at the same tick range (common in popular pools)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: pool.positions(key) returns aggregated liquidity for ALL NFTs at that tick range, not the specific tokenId's liquidity
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Should use positionManager.positions(tokenId) to get per-NFT liquidity, then calculate amounts from that
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-NFT liquidity query; tokenId is decoded but only used for position lookup, not liquidity calculation

analysis_questions: How much external liquidity typically exists at the same tick ranges in popular pools?
What is the maximum TVL inflation possible?
Can an attacker deliberately add liquidity at the same tick range to inflate vault TVL?
validation_requirements: Open a UNIv3 position via the connector
Add external liquidity at the same tick range via a different account
Compare reported TVL with actual vault-owned liquidity

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker observes the vault's UNIv3 position tick range, then adds liquidity at the same tick range through the same positionManager. The vault's TVL inflates by the attacker's liquidity amount. The attacker then deposits into the vault at the inflated share price to dilute existing holders, or existing holders withdraw at inflated prices. Alternatively, this occurs naturally in popular pools where many positions overlap.
prerequisites: Vault holds at least one UNIv3 position
Other liquidity exists at the same tick range

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inflated share price enables existing depositors to withdraw more than their fair share; new depositors receive fewer shares than deserved
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: New depositors lose value; existing depositors can extract excess value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires fixing the TVL calculation to use per-NFT liquidity

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing per-NFT liquidity vs aggregated pool position liquidity

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Attacker needs liquidity to add to same tick range (proportional to inflation)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction or passive (existing external liquidity)

threats.title: SwapAndBridgeHandler onlyExistingRoute Modifier Allows Disabled Routes to Execute
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyExistingRoute modifier uses AND (&&) logic: `if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert`. This only reverts when the route address is ZERO AND the route is disabled. A disabled route with a non-zero address passes the check. Since executeSwap and executeBridge never independently verify isEnabled, the emergency disable mechanism (setEnableRoute) is ineffective.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a route is compromised or deprecated, the maintainer/emergency role disables it via setEnableRoute. But the disabled route can still be used for swaps and bridges because the modifier logic is wrong. This bypasses a critical safety mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert RouteNotFound(); - uses && instead of ||, allowing disabled routes with non-zero addresses to pass

preconditions: A route exists with a non-zero address
The route has been disabled via setEnableRoute(routeId, false)
An eligible user attempts to use the disabled route

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: AND logic in condition should be OR: route == address(0) || !isEnabled
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Disabled routes remain functional, defeating the emergency disable mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No secondary isEnabled check in executeSwap or executeBridge

analysis_questions: Are there scenarios where a route needs to be emergency-disabled?
Can a disabled route be exploited to drain funds?
Does verifyRoute also have this bug?
validation_requirements: Add a route, disable it, then attempt executeSwap - should fail but won't
Verify that setEnableRoute(routeId, false) has no practical effect

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A swap route is found to have a vulnerability or is compromised. The emergency role disables it via setEnableRoute. However, eligible users (connectors) can still execute swaps through the disabled route because the modifier doesn't properly check the isEnabled flag. Funds routed through the compromised route are at risk.
prerequisites: At least one route added with a non-zero address
Route subsequently disabled

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Emergency disable mechanism is ineffective; compromised routes remain usable, potentially draining swap funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any vault using the handler for swaps/bridges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Must remove the route entirely or set its address to address(0)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for disabled route rejection

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be verified by attempting swap on disabled route

threats.title: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition Passes removePosition=true Instead of false, Losing Position Tracking
threats.hypothesis_description: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition calls `registry.updateHoldingPosition(vaultId, ..., abi.encode(accountId), "", true)` with `removePosition=true`. This attempts to REMOVE a position when the intent is to ADD one. Since the position doesn't exist yet (first call returns positionIndex=0), the removal is a no-op (returns type(uint256).max). The borrow position is never tracked in the registry.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wrong boolean flag causes borrow positions to be invisible to the TVL calculation. Borrowed amounts and associated risks are not tracked, leading to incorrect TVL and potential hidden leverage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: registry.updateHoldingPosition(vaultId, registry.calculatePositionId(address(this), DOL_POSITION_ID, ""), abi.encode(accountId), "", true); - passes true (remove) instead of false (add)

preconditions: Manager calls openBorrowPosition on DolomiteConnector

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Compare with deposit() at line 33 which correctly passes false; compare with closeBorrowPosition which also passes true (correct for closing)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that position was actually registered after creation

analysis_questions: Does the missing position tracking affect TVL calculations?
Can the borrow position ever be properly closed if it was never tracked?
Are there other boolean flag errors in the codebase?
validation_requirements: Call openBorrowPosition and verify registry state
Check if _getPositionTVL accounts for the borrow position
Verify closeBorrowPosition behavior when position was never added

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager opens a Dolomite borrow position. The position is not tracked in the registry, so TVL calculation misses it. If the borrow creates debt, the vault's TVL is overstated (debt not subtracted). This leads to inflated share prices, allowing withdrawals of more value than the vault actually holds.
prerequisites: DolomiteConnector deployed and active
Manager opens a borrow position

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Untracked debt leads to overstated TVL, enabling excess withdrawals that drain the vault
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors - remaining depositors bear the untracked debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix; position may need manual registry insertion

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for position registry state after openBorrowPosition

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Inspect holdingPositions array after openBorrowPosition call

threats.title: SiloConnector TVL Calculation Incorrect for Non-18-Decimal Tokens Due to Hardcoded 1e18 Normalization
threats.hypothesis_description: SiloConnector._getPositionTVL calculates TVL using `_getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18)` as a price-per-unit, then multiplies by balance and divides by 1e18. This assumes all token balances are in 18-decimal format. For tokens with 6 decimals (USDC, USDT), the balance is 1e12x smaller than expected relative to the 1e18 normalization, producing a TVL that is 1e12x too small. Conversely, for tokens with more than 18 decimals, TVL would be inflated.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDC and USDT are among the most commonly used tokens in DeFi lending protocols. Using them with Silo through this connector would produce wildly incorrect TVL values, directly affecting share prices.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 price = _getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18); totalDepositAmount += depositAmount * price / 1e18; totalBAmount += borrowAmount * price / 1e18; - assumes all token balances are 18-decimal

preconditions: SiloConnector holds positions involving non-18-decimal tokens (e.g., USDC, USDT with 6 decimals)
TVL calculation is triggered

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hardcoded 1e18 normalization regardless of token decimals; depositAmount for USDC is in 6-decimal scale but normalized against 1e18 price
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For USDC (6 decimals): 1000 USDC = 1000e6 balance. Price = _getValue(USDC, base, 1e18). Result = 1000e6 * price / 1e18, which is 1e12 times too small
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No token decimal lookup; no scaling to normalize different decimal tokens

analysis_questions: What tokens are actually used with SiloConnector in deployment?
How does _getValue handle decimal differences internally?
Is the valueOracle expected to return values normalized to a specific decimal?
validation_requirements: Deposit USDC (6 decimals) into Silo via connector
Calculate TVL and compare with expected value
Verify the factor of error (should be ~1e12 for 6-decimal tokens)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A vault uses SiloConnector with USDC positions. The TVL for these positions is underreported by ~1e12x. This makes shares appear cheaper than they should be, allowing an attacker to deposit and acquire a disproportionately large share of the vault, then trigger a correct TVL recalculation or withdraw via other connectors with correct pricing.
prerequisites: SiloConnector active with non-18-decimal token positions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Massively incorrect TVL for non-18-decimal token positions; share price manipulation enabling value extraction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to properly handle token decimals

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test with non-18-decimal tokens in SiloConnector TVL calculation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare calculated TVL with manual calculation for known positions

threats.title: SNXv3Connector TVL Double-Counts Collateral by Adding totalDeposited and totalAssigned
threats.hypothesis_description: SNXv3Connector._getPositionTVL calculates TVL as `_getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned)`. In SNX V3, `getAccountCollateral` returns (totalDeposited, totalAssigned, totalLocked) where totalDeposited is the total collateral in the account and totalAssigned is the portion delegated to pools (a subset of totalDeposited). Adding them double-counts the delegated portion.
threats.investigation_rationale: If totalAssigned is a subset of totalDeposited (as documented in SNX V3), the TVL inflation is exactly totalAssigned. This inflates share prices proportionally to delegated collateral.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (uint256 totalDeposited, uint256 totalAssigned, uint256 totalLocked) = SNXCoreProxy.getAccountCollateral(accountId, collateralType); tvl = _getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned);

preconditions: SNXV3Connector has deposited and delegated collateral
totalAssigned > 0 (collateral delegated to a pool)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalDeposited + totalAssigned where totalAssigned is a subset of totalDeposited
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Should use just totalDeposited, or totalDeposited + unrealized rewards if applicable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No documentation of SNX V3 getAccountCollateral return value semantics in the code

analysis_questions: Confirm SNX V3 getAccountCollateral semantics: is totalAssigned a subset of totalDeposited?
What is the magnitude of overcounting for typical delegated positions?
Does totalLocked need to be considered?
validation_requirements: Deposit and delegate via SNX V3, then compare getAccountCollateral values
Verify if totalDeposited >= totalAssigned always holds
Compare connector TVL with actual position value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault deposits and delegates collateral in SNX V3. TVL is inflated by the delegated amount. Share price is artificially high, allowing existing holders to withdraw more than their fair share.
prerequisites: SNXV3Connector active with delegated collateral

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: TVL inflated by up to 2x the delegated collateral amount, enabling excess withdrawals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: New depositors receive fewer shares; existing depositors can extract excess value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to use correct TVL formula

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing connector TVL with actual SNX V3 position value

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare getAccountCollateral values against known deposit amounts

threats.title: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity Empty Implementation Provides Zero Withdrawal Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity is declared as `function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { }` with an empty body. This function is called by BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress when the accounting manager requests token transfers. The Watcher security layer, designed to validate withdrawal amounts and prevent unauthorized fund extraction, provides no actual verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Watcher role is documented as a critical safety mechanism to monitor and revert suspicious actions. With an empty verification function, any withdrawal amount requested by the accounting manager passes without scrutiny. The entire safety layer designed to prevent malicious or erroneous withdrawals is absent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { } - empty function body

preconditions: Accounting manager calls sendTokensToTrustedAddress on any connector
The watcher contract is the deployed Watchers.sol (which has the empty function)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Empty function body for security-critical verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: sendTokensToTrustedAddress at BaseConnector.sol trusts the newAmount from data parameter without any watcher validation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No comparison between withdrawAmount and sentAmount, no bounds checking, no signature verification

analysis_questions: Was this intended as a placeholder for future implementation?
Are there other safety mechanisms that compensate for the missing verification?
Could the Watchers contract be a multisig that verifies off-chain?
validation_requirements: Trace the sendTokensToTrustedAddress accounting manager path
Verify that newAmount from data parameter is used without bounds checking
Confirm no other verification occurs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised or buggy accounting manager calls sendTokensToTrustedAddress with an inflated newAmount in the data parameter. The Watcher verification does nothing, and the connector transfers the requested amount. The attacker drains connector funds through the accounting manager path.
prerequisites: Compromised accounting manager or keeper that can trigger withdrawals

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: The documented safety mechanism for withdrawal verification is completely absent; any withdrawal amount passes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors - funds can be extracted without verification
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires implementing actual verification logic in the Watchers contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test verifying that watcher actually validates withdrawal amounts

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Applicable to every withdrawal through accounting manager
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Code inspection immediately reveals the empty function

threats.title: Cross-Chain TVL Update Lacks Monotonic Timestamp Check Allowing Stale Data to Overwrite Fresh Data
threats.hypothesis_description: OmnichainManagerBaseChain.updateTVL accepts any updateTime parameter without checking if it is newer than the existing position timestamp. If LayerZero delivers messages out of order (older message arrives after newer one), a stale TVL value overwrites a more recent one. There is no monotonic timestamp enforcement.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cross-chain message delivery is not guaranteed to be ordered. If a delayed message with old TVL data arrives after a fresher update has been applied, it replaces the current TVL with outdated data. This can cause share price miscalculation during the period the stale data is active.

proof_of_signal.pattern: registry.updateHoldingPostionWithTime(vaultId, ..., updateTime); - no comparison with existing positionTimestamp before overwriting

preconditions: Vault has omnichain positions with TVL reported from other chains
Two or more TVL update messages sent in quick succession
LayerZero delivers the messages out of order

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: updateTime accepted without comparison to existing timestamp
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No check like `if (updateTime <= existingTimestamp) revert` to enforce monotonicity

analysis_questions: How often does LayerZero deliver messages out of order in practice?
How long would stale data persist before being corrected?
Can an attacker force out-of-order delivery?
validation_requirements: Send two TVL updates with different timestamps
Deliver them in reverse order
Verify that the older update overwrites the newer one

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: TVL update for 1M is sent at T=100. TVL update for 500K is sent at T=99 (due to rapid succession). If the T=99 message arrives after T=100, the position shows 500K instead of 1M, deflating TVL. An attacker deposits at the deflated price, then the correct TVL is restored, and the attacker withdraws at the higher correct price.
prerequisites: Multi-chain vault deployment
Multiple TVL update messages in flight

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary share price manipulation via stale cross-chain TVL data
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Depositors and withdrawers during the stale data window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Corrected on next TVL update with newer timestamp

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for out-of-order message delivery handling

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Proportional to share price manipulation profit
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block; depends on LZ delivery timing

threats.title: BaseConnector.transferPositionToAnotherConnector Silently Succeeds When Target Connector Is Inactive
threats.hypothesis_description: transferPositionToAnotherConnector checks `if (registry.isAnActiveConnector(vaultId, connector))` but does nothing when the check fails—no revert, no error. The function silently succeeds, and the keeper believes the position was transferred successfully when in reality nothing happened. Token amounts are not moved, but the operation is considered complete.
threats.investigation_rationale: Silent failures in critical financial operations are dangerous. If a connector is deactivated between the time a strategy is planned and executed, the position transfer silently fails. The keeper's strategy assumptions become invalid, potentially leading to incorrect risk management or stranded positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (registry.isAnActiveConnector(vaultId, connector)) { IConnector(connector).addLiquidity(tokens, amounts, data); } - no else branch or revert for inactive connector

preconditions: Target connector is inactive (disabled in registry)
Manager calls transferPositionToAnotherConnector

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if-without-else on critical operation; no revert on failure path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Function emits TransferPositionToConnector event regardless of whether transfer actually happened

analysis_questions: Can a deactivated connector cause strategy inconsistencies?
Does the keeper rely on the success of this function for subsequent operations?
Are there compensating mechanisms to detect failed transfers?
validation_requirements: Call transferPositionToAnotherConnector with an inactive connector
Verify function succeeds without reverting
Verify no tokens were actually moved

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A connector is deactivated as part of emergency response. The keeper then attempts to rebalance positions by transferring to this connector. The call succeeds silently, tokens remain in the source connector, but the strategy assumes they've moved. Subsequent operations based on this assumption may fail or produce incorrect results.
prerequisites: Target connector inactive in registry
Manager attempts position transfer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Strategy execution becomes unreliable; positions may be stranded or double-counted in strategy logic
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Vault depositors affected by incorrect strategy execution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Detectable through balance monitoring; recoverable by re-executing transfers

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for inactive connector transfer behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare pre/post balances to detect silent failure

threats.title: BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan Allows Arbitrary External Calls to Unvalidated Destination Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: In receiveFlashLoan, the `destinationConnector[]` addresses are called with arbitrary calldata and gas limits without any validation against the registry. Only the `receiver` address is checked as an active connector. The keeper (via multisig) can direct flash-loaned funds to any contract, bypassing connector-specific safety checks and potentially interacting with unaudited or malicious contracts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The flash loan mechanism extends the keeper's execution authority beyond the defined connector interface. While keeper access requires multisig compromise (LOW realism), the lack of target validation means a compromised keeper can execute arbitrary contract interactions using flash-loaned capital.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool success,) = destinationConnector[i].call{ value: 0, gas: gas[i] }(callingData[i]); - destinationConnector[i] is from userData, never validated against registry

preconditions: Caller stored in BalancerFlashLoan must be the keeperContract
Receiver must be an active connector in the registry
Keeper multisig must sign the flash loan transaction (requires threshold signatures)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Arbitrary .call to unvalidated addresses with user-supplied calldata
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: destinationConnector array decoded from userData is never checked: `(bool success,) = destinationConnector[i].call{...}(callingData[i])` where destinationConnector[i] can be ANY address
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing `require(registry.isAnActiveConnector(vaultId, destinationConnector[i]))` check before each call

analysis_questions: Can the arbitrary call targets interact with connector token balances?
What damage can be done via arbitrary calls from the BalancerFlashLoan contract?
Are there approval states on the BalancerFlashLoan that could be exploited?
validation_requirements: Construct a flash loan that targets a non-connector contract
Verify the call executes without validation
Assess what actions the BalancerFlashLoan contract can perform via these calls

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised keeper multisig constructs a flash loan with destinationConnector pointing to arbitrary contracts. The flash loan sends tokens to a legitimate receiver connector, but then executes arbitrary calls (e.g., approve tokens on the receiver to an attacker-controlled address). The receiver's tokens are then drained in a separate transaction.
prerequisites: Compromised keeper multisig (requires threshold signatures)
Flash loan availability

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Arbitrary contract interaction capability via flash loan; potential fund drainage from connectors via approval manipulation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors if flash loan is used maliciously
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on specific exploitation; may require emergency pause

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for destinationConnector address validation in flash loan path

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Verify gas limits are sufficient for arbitrary calls
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Flash loan amount proportional to exploitation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor flash loan events and call traces for unexpected target contracts
